messages.author: ClinchSparkleBiscuit
messages.text: Interesting Results: I switch Golden Gate and Old Millard to a spread factor of 8 for a trace path test. I got a 50% (3 of 6) success rate on a trace route compared to a spread factor of 7 where I had failed 20 of 20 attempts. (edited)
messages.content_html: Interesting Results: I switch Golden Gate and Old Millard to a spread factor of 8 for a trace path test. I got a 50% (3 of 6) success rate on a trace route compared to a spread factor of 7 where I had failed 20 of 20 attempts. (edited)
messages.reactions_html: 1

messages.author: nightcrawler
messages.text: MT LongFast has a link budget of 153dB with data rate of 1.07kbps. MC recommend preset as has a link budget of 149dB with a data rate of 2.73kbps. Increasing it to SF 8 gives it a link budget of 152dB and 1.56kbps. The "deprecated" preset in MC matches MTs LongFast.
messages.content_html: MT LongFast has a link budget of 153dB with data rate of 1.07kbps. MC recommend preset as has a link budget of 149dB with a data rate of 2.73kbps. Increasing it to SF 8 gives it a link budget of 152dB and 1.56kbps. The "deprecated" preset in MC matches MTs LongFast.

messages.author: nightcrawler
messages.text: Page I used for those numbers - https://unsigned.io/understanding-lora-parameters/
messages.content_html: Page I used for those numbers - https://unsigned.io/understanding-lora-parameters/
embeds_html:
LoRa bitrate calculator and understanding LoRa parameters
It can be pretty useful to know how LoRa parameters influence data rate and range, for example when configuring your RNode. In this post I'll go over the basic concepts needed to understand the different LoRa parameters, and how they affect range and data rate.
messages.reply_html:
nightcrawler
MT LongFast has a link budget of 153dB with data rate of 1.07kbps. MC recommend preset as has a link budget of 149dB with a data rate of 2.73kbps. Increasing it to SF 8 gives it a link budget of 152dB and 1.56kbps. The "deprecated" preset in MC matches MTs LongFast.

messages.author: ClinchSparkleBiscuit
messages.text: I think using the same lora settings as Long Fast would be worth a try on MC. The real problem, I think, was the meshtastic protocol on top of LORA. The flood routing caused congestion and also the repeaters were snagging hops. I am trying things out to see how they go on a couple of repeaters that have a fringe connection. Having fun experimenting.
messages.content_html: I think using the same lora settings as Long Fast would be worth a try on MC. The real problem, I think, was the meshtastic protocol on top of LORA. Having fun experimenting.

messages.author: [AL0R] Matt
messages.text: While it is a single sweep, the bandwidth equates to the chip rate. 62.5khz and sf7 means that we have 488 symbols per sec. If we increased to 125khz, that increases the symbols per sec to 977. This means that as the band width doubles (and/or the SF reduces by 1), the time to send halves. Thus the signal is shorter and more suceptible to interference.
messages.content_html: While it is a single sweep, the bandwidth equates to the chip rate. 62.5khz and sf7 means that we have 488 symbols per sec. If we increased to 125khz, that increases the symbols per sec to 977. This means that as the band width doubles (and/or the SF reduces by 1), the time to send halves. Thus the signal is shorter and more suceptible to interference.

messages.author: ClinchSparkleBiscuit
messages.text: Yes, but does the bandwidth have any effect on power density?
messages.content_html: Yes, but does the bandwidth have any effect on power density?

messages.author: [AL0R] Matt
messages.text: Not as compared to "fm" or "am". But by stretching the length of the symbol, we make it "stronger" (even though there is no actual increase in the density.
messages.content_html: Not as compared to "fm" or "am". But by stretching the length of the symbol, we make it "stronger" (even though there is no actual increase in the density.

messages.author: [AL0R] Matt
messages.text: Foregoing the symbol timing.... a receiver that has to "hear" a wider bandwidth, hears "more" signal - including signal that isn't yours. Using the sx1262 chipset.... the receiver has a sensitivity rating of -124db for a 125khz channel (sf7) and -121 for a 250khz channel (sf7). Thus the receiver "loses" 3db of sensitivity every time you double the bandwidth. (edited)
messages.content_html: Foregoing the symbol timing.... a receiver that has to "hear" a wider bandwidth, hears "more" signal - including signal that isn't yours. Using the sx1262 chipset.... the receiver has a sensitivity rating of -124db for a 125khz channel (sf7) and -121 for a 250khz channel (sf7). Thus the receiver "loses" 3db of sensitivity every time you double the bandwidth. (edited)

messages.reply_html:
[AL0R] Matt
If we increase the sf to 12 on a 250 channel, we "recover" not only the lost signal, but some extra, moving the sensitivity to -134dbm. We do this though, at the cost of massively reduced throughput. We have stretched the length of the symbols out so long, that it takes 16ms to send 1 symbol. A 10 byte payload takes 450ms of transmit at sf12. At sf7, it takes 21ms. Moving to sf8 = 42ms.

messages.author: Rusty Chinook
messages.text: There are probably 20-30 MT nodes that I see pretty regularly in Lincoln (up from about 6-10 last year), but nothing that I would call a solidly linked mesh. FWIW, I did deploy a MC repeater with a reasonably decent antenna for a couple of days last week. I was a bit discouraged by the much diminished range using the default settings compared to MT LF...sent me down a rabbit hole of checking firmware, hardware, and antennas and side-by-side range comparisons. To be fair, that effort probably wasn't as charged as it could have been... my goals for using the mesh are partially related to the ability to use serial communications to automate sending text, which is not (yet) supported in the pre-compiled MC firmware and companion app. Watching this "experiment" play out in Omaha has been quite enlightening. I do have a couple of solar nodes built and ready to deploy, but as rapidly as things are changing up the road, I'm not in any hurry to put them out just yet. (edited)

messages.author: Mike / N2QDK
messages.text: Not exactly. We know the shortcomings of MT's "managed" flood: It often manages to make poor decisions on what messages to flood and when, which compromises real-world effectiveness and reach of public channel messages. MC message delivery tends to be more robust because it's a true flood - and there's work being done to help with the timing of flood message sending with repeaters listening for free airtime and repeater owners being able to txdelay as well as a bit of randomness for when to transmit. MC is also a less chatty protocol overall by design - more free airtime means less potential packet collisions. (edited)
messages.content_html: Not exactly. We know the shortcomings of MT's "managed" flood: It often manages to make poor decisions on what messages to flood and when, which compromises real-world effectiveness and reach of public channel messages. MC message delivery tends to be more robust because it's a true flood - and there's work being done to help with the timing of flood message sending with repeaters listening for free airtime and repeater owners being able to txdelay as well as a bit of randomness for when to transmit. MC is also a less chatty protocol overall by design - more free airtime means less potential packet collisions. (edited)

We know the shortcomings of MT's "managed" flood: It often manages to make poor decisions on what messages to flood and when, which compromises real-world effectiveness and reach of public channel messages. MC message delivery tends to be more robust because it's a true flood - and there's work being done to help with the timing of flood message sending with repeaters listening for free airtime and repeater owners being able to txdelay as well as a bit of randomness for when to transmit. MC is also a less chatty protocol overall by design - more free airtime means less potential packet collisions. (edited)

messages.author: Mike / N2QDK
messages.text: Ferrites being the latest suggestion, on top of known tried and true stuff like shielding and grounding that I’ll be trying. There’s multiple nearby (physical and frequency) radio systems between 870 and 890MHz at -45dB that cavity filters can’t quite knock back. On MC, rx is 6-9 dB deaf than xmit at 22dBm. Open to any and all suggestions for mitigation.
messages.content_html: Ferrites being the latest suggestion, on top of known tried and true stuff like shielding and grounding that I’ll be trying. There’s multiple nearby (physical and frequency) radio systems between 870 and 890MHz at -45dB that cavity filters can’t quite knock back. On MC, rx is 6-9 dB deaf than xmit at 22dBm. Open to any and all suggestions for mitigation.
messages.reply_html:
[AL0R] Matt
It's very rare that the use of a ferrite has any impact in a digital system such as this. If your power is "that" noisy, please deal with the source, rather than trying to mitigate it downstream.

messages.author: Scott Redd
messages.text: Here's an email I sent to folks who contacted me via the OmaMesh.net web site. ---- Hi there! Thanks again for reaching out through OmaMesh.net. Here’s a quick update on what’s been happening locally. Meshtastic & MeshCore Meshtastic is still alive and working great. That said, many local users — including some with access to tower sites and pro gear — have been moving to MeshCore because messaging and ACK reliability tend to be better, especially over multiple hops. If you want to try MeshCore, jump into Public Chat, send a message to any Room Server, or just DM someone on the air. Most folks are happy to respond. MeshBot I’m also running a small MeshBot that can respond to pings, act as a Magic 8 Ball, and list nearby stations it has heard. Feel free to interact with it if you see it. Recommended Radio Settings Local testing shows SF9 works better than the default SF7. To match Omaha settings: Preset: USA/Canada (Recommended) Spreading Factor: 9 If you run a repeater: Coding Rate: 8 Save and reboot These settings have noticeably improved metro-wide consistency. Distance & Performance We’ve seen some impressive paths lately: Nearly 60 miles of reliable multi-hop delivery A confirmed 33-mile hop from Council Bluffs → Mead Interactive chats between downtown Omaha ↔ Elkhorn, often with positive ACKs A fun #tictactoe hashtag channel for playing the game entirely over RF Join the Local Discords Meshtastic Omaha: https://discordapp.com/channels/867578229534359593/1217697721285218314 MeshCore Omaha: https://discordapp.com/channels/1343693475589263471/1439732912672211024 Both are friendly places to meet others and ask questions. Website OmaMesh.net will eventually have more content, so feel free to check in from time to time. If you have questions or want help configuring a device or placing a node, just reply — we’re always glad to welcome new operators. Hope to see you on the air! — Scott OmaMesh.net (often on MeshCore as Red X2) (edited)

messages.author: Rusty Chinook
messages.text: You probably already know about this so it may be more of a comment for others looking to do something similar, but I just upgraded a rak connected by USB to a SoC board with wifi located in an awkward location using adafruit-nrfutil: adafruit-nrfutil --verbose dfu serial --package RAK_4631_repeater-v1.11.0-6d32193.zip -p /dev/ttyACM0 -b 115200 --singlebank --touch 1200 I didn't need to push any button for upgrades and could connect via terminal emulator after upgrading without issue. You can also do ESP-based boards with: esptool.py -p /dev/ttyUSB0 --chip esp32 write_flash 0x00000 Heltec_v2_companion_radio_ble-v1.11.0-6d32193.bin Here's some further guidance: https://deepwiki.com/ripplebiz/MeshCore/10.1-installation-and-flashing . Use at your own risk, which, in Matt's case, seems to be substantially elevated . Some handy CLI commands if you can connect to the node over USB using a terminal emulator: https://deepwiki.com/ripplebiz/MeshCore/10.2-basic-configuration (edited)
messages.content_html: You probably already know about this so it may be more of a comment for others looking to do something similar, but I just upgraded a rak connected by USB to a SoC board with wifi located in an awkward location using adafruit-nrfutil: adafruit-nrfutil --verbose dfu serial --package RAK_4631_repeater-v1.11.0-6d32193.zip -p /dev/ttyACM0 -b 115200 --singlebank --touch 1200 I didn't need to push any button for upgrades and could connect via terminal emulator after upgrading without issue. You can also do ESP-based boards with: esptool.py -p /dev/ttyUSB0 --chip esp32 write_flash 0x00000 Heltec_v2_companion_radio_ble-v1.11.0-6d32193.bin Here's some further guidance: https://deepwiki.com/ripplebiz/MeshCore/10.1-installation-and-flashing. Use at your own risk, which, in Matt's case, seems to be substantially elevated . Some handy CLI commands if you can connect to the node over USB using a terminal emulator: https://deepwiki.com/ripplebiz/MeshCore/10.2-basic-configuration (edited)

messages.author: Pyromaniac3814
messages.text: Does coding rate change compatibility with the mesh as a whole? Or can I have whatever coding rate without seperating myself from the mesh?
messages.content_html: Does coding rate change compatibility with the mesh as a whole? Or can I have whatever coding rate without seperating myself from the mesh?

messages.author: nightcrawler
messages.text: You can have a different coding rate and still be on the same mesh. You mainly need to have the same frequency, bandwidth, and spreading factor as everyone else. You'll want to use the smallest CR that you can reliably get away with.
messages.content_html: You can have a different coding rate and still be on the same mesh. You mainly need to have the same frequency, bandwidth, and spreading factor as everyone else. You'll want to use the smallest CR that you can reliably get away with.
messages.reply_html:
Pyromaniac3814
Does coding rate change compatibility with the mesh as a whole? Or can I have whatever coding rate without seperating myself from the mesh?

messages.author: Pyromaniac3814
messages.text: Sweet, thanks!
messages.content_html: Sweet, thanks!
messages.reply_html:
nightcrawler
You can have a different coding rate and still be on the same mesh. You mainly need to have the same frequency, bandwidth, and spreading factor as everyone else. You'll want to use the smallest CR that you can reliably get away with.

Once this is cascaded with a window filter I have, I hope that the spur is reduced enough that this parameter combination works "ok" at this site. (edited)
messages.content_html: Here is the primary pass filter for NOMA, showing the current frequency we are running, along with the higher and lower 3db markers, and finally, marker 3 showing the frequency we were using for MT. Once this is cascaded with a window filter I have, I hope that the spur is reduced enough that this parameter combination works "ok" at this site. (edited)

messages.author: [AL0R] Matt
messages.text: I showed the full width of the 900 ISM band for scale. For comparison, when you buy a "full band" band pass filter, the entire width of this graph is passed. I've seen conversations mentioning the "25MHz" and "7 MHz" filters someone on Discord bought... the 25MHZ would be almost the full ISM band. Since this band is SO noisy, there is really no point to even using a filter that passes so much. You really need to dial them in, which just isn't the point of the chinesium products.

messages.author: Scott Redd
messages.text: The orange path lines on the OmaMesh map show how my MeshBot's node believes it can reach other nodes on the network. MeshCore doesn't broadcast a global routing table, so each device only knows the best routes from its own perspective. The bot listens to advertisements, DMs, channel messages, and path-related events, then reconstructs the hop sequence (like 0f → 1a → fb) by matching those hop IDs to real nodes that have recently broadcast their location. This gives us a clear picture of how routing looks from a well-connected listening post in the network. Even though these routes come from just one node’s viewpoint, they’re still valuable to the whole community. They reveal which repeaters are currently acting as main forwarders, how distant nodes tie into the Omaha cluster, and when paths shift or degrade—something most users can’t see from the phone app alone. Think of the map as a “routing weather radar”: it isn’t the whole truth everywhere, but it’s an accurate and useful snapshot that helps everyone understand mesh behavior in real time.

messages.author: Scott Redd
messages.text: One idea would be to distribute a MeshBot script to folks who want to run one. You'd need a dedicated radio, and BLE or USB connection to your computer (desktop, server, raspberry pi). The script could write to a cloud database and a viewer on the omamesh website could render maps from the database.
messages.content_html: One idea would be to distribute a MeshBot script to folks who want to run one. You'd need a dedicated radio, and BLE or USB connection to your computer (desktop, server, raspberry pi). The script could write to a cloud database and a viewer on the omamesh website could render maps from the database.

messages.text: I'm glad you're have a good experience so far! I'd recommend the 60 min zero hop advert and 3 hour flood advert for your repeaters. And for your companion, just send one manually whenever you are using the app and think about it. Traffic is lighter, by design, on MeshCore, and it doesn't really gum things up when you advert. (edited)

messages.author: [AL0R] Matt
messages.text: Here's a great example of "noise" in the metro area. I live less than 100y away from a water plant, and they run a 900 ISM FHSS bridge between the plant and water tower, as well as 900 meter collection from the site. Previously, my yagi aimed right at it. Now, I'm about 110 degrees off axis. You can see on the noise floor graph when I swung the yagi yesterday at 230pm. I still here other devices, but I'm nulling out around 20db of the plant transmitter.
messages.content_html: Here's a great example of "noise" in the metro area. I live less than 100y away from a water plant, and they run a 900 ISM FHSS bridge between the plant and water tower, as well as 900 meter collection from the site. Previously, my yagi aimed right at it. Now, I'm about 110 degrees off axis. You can see on the noise floor graph when I swung the yagi yesterday at 230pm. I still here other devices, but I'm nulling out around 20db of the plant transmitter.

messages.author: montazuma
messages.text: Welcome! I'm far from the expert but I'll give you my two cents on getting started that I wish I would have realized earlier. Look at getting yourself a repeater device and companion device, having both off the bat makes participating at the start much easier and imho makes it easier to learn what you're doing. A solar powered repeater high up in the air is the ideal, but my advice would be to not fret the ideal out the gate and just get something setup. Even if that first repeater is a Heltec v3 taped to a window running off a wall outlet. From personal experience, your best served to just get started and build up to more.
messages.content_html:

Even if that first repeater is a Heltec v3 taped to a window running off a wall outlet. From personal experience, your best served to just get started and build up to more.

messages.author: amc4550
messages.text: Yeah, the performance in Bellevue hasn't been great. I hate to say it, but I've been underwhelmed by meshcore overall. I still like the routing protocol, but the Bluetooth connectivity issues are annoying and all the nodes I've tested have behaved erratically at one point or another. Makes it hard to determine how much is RF/topography problems vs software/hardware related.
messages.content_html: Yeah, the performance in Bellevue hasn't been great. I hate to say it, but I've been underwhelmed by meshcore overall. I still like the routing protocol, but the Bluetooth connectivity issues are annoying and all the nodes I've tested have behaved erratically at one point or another. Makes it hard to determine how much is RF/topography problems vs software/hardware related.

messages.author: PickleKing
messages.text: AMC, What hardware are you having Bluetooth trouble on? My raks have been flawless. My tbeam usually takes two tries to connect. But other than that it’s been good. As far as the network, I’ve seen comparable or better results with mesh core through out most of the areas I operate in daily. And given that Bellevue’s really does have difficult topography, I think that’s more likely your issue. But it is hard to be sure. (edited)

messages.author: PickleKing
messages.text: I think a fear of heights is reasonable. For anybody in the group that does want a roof install, I’ve found that a coupler and short piece of pvc pipe allows you to pre-assemble everything and the install is simple. Every house has a 3-4” vent stack. And with another 12” of pipe you have plenty of room to mount a node and small solar panel. Install is easy and can be removed just as easy. And you don’t have to spend much time on the roof fiddling with mounting hardware or power tools.
messages.content_html: I think a fear of heights is reasonable. For anybody in the group that does want a roof install, I’ve found that a coupler and short piece of pvc pipe allows you to pre-assemble everything and the install is simple. Every house has a 3-4” vent stack. And with another 12” of pipe you have plenty of room to mount a node and small solar panel. Install is easy and can be removed just as easy. And you don’t have to spend much time on the roof fiddling with mounting hardware or power tools.

messages.text: I also turned the power way down on a node and threw it in a metal box to simulate a weak connection. No real difference between SF8 & SF9. But SF10 significantly improved reliability. messages.content_html: I also turned the power way down on a node and threw it in a metal box to simulate a weak connection. No real difference between SF8 & SF9. Add CLI command to regenerate key pair by mattzzw · Pull Request #...
In a situation where repeaters face zero hop key prefix collisions, keys will have to be regenerated. Doing this via the set prv.key command is only possible via physical connection to the repeater...

messages.author: amc4550
messages.text: Seems a common issue with the path routing is when you have nodes along the way with lopsided RX/TX performance. For example, my T1000 can pick some things up indoors without the repeater, but then when sending a message out it struggles if it bypasses the repeater.

messages.author: [AL0R] Matt
messages.text: "Most" LPDAs are driven from the front, where the shortest element determines the highest usable frequency. LPDA elements are cross phased, which is why there are two "booms". Both booms are "hot" even though only one is connected to the center conductor. While both are also connected to ground, the RF radiates as it is an ac coupled signal, not dc. An LPD only has "3" elements in use at any given time. A director, the driven, and the reflector. The driven element is determined by the resonance at the frequency of interest.
messages.content_html: "Most" LPDAs are driven from the front, where the shortest element determines the highest usable frequency. LPDA elements are cross phased, which is why there are two "booms". Both booms are "hot" even though only one is connected to the center conductor. While both are also connected to ground, the RF radiates as it is an ac coupled signal, not dc. An LPD only has "3" elements in use at any given time. A director, the driven, and the reflector. The driven element is determined by the resonance at the frequency of interest.

messages.author: [AL0R] Matt
messages.text: As to the magic of timmos mod... it would take a real antenna range to determine how effective it is. LPDAs are very balanced antennas, and adding an odd element may lower the swr at a given frequency, but may also distort the pattern significantly. Additionally, the difference in element diameter can have an impact on overall performance as well. The large diameter elements contribute to wider bandwidth. While we are concerned with a very specific frequency, it may work fine, at the cost of "wide band" performance.

LPDAs are very balanced antennas, and adding an odd element may lower the swr at a given frequency, but may also distort the pattern significantly. Additionally, the difference in element diameter can have an impact on overall performance as well. The large diameter elements contribute to wider bandwidth. While we are concerned with a very specific frequency, it may work fine, at the cost of "wide band" performance.

messages.author: amc4550
messages.text: Ok so digging around on different channels, it sounds like this is a fairly widespread problem on SX1262 receivers where some sort of AGC "lockup" can be triggered by a strong lora signal on nearby frequencies. I'm running MC, MT, and Lorawan on the same roof ~10-20ft apart which is probably a factor.

messages.author: PickleKing
messages.text: It’s that quick elevation climb of about 100 feet that then falls off again when going north or west of downtown Bellevue. The only way to beat that with nodes at home/garage height will be to get one on top of the edge of that bowl you’re inside. I bet when you drive west out of town you’re picking up your desired repeaters around the Belleaire elementary school area. Side note, I keep noticing omaha has their schools on really good spots for LOS coms. I suppose they have some kind of district system that relies on LOS. Otherwise your options are something south, preferably well past the base, or something east in the loess hills. Or something taller like I’m working on to the north. Lastly, you could make nice with someone that lives on the rim of the “Bellevue Bowl”. There are houses on a few good spots and there’s stuff like the city pool or event center that would be modestly acceptable.

messages.author: [AL0R] Matt
messages.text: Actually, no. Many schools are on prominences for no purpose, other than personal choice. In decades past, OPS (and other school districts), did and do use microwave between some facilities. I've installed and subsequently removed much of this infrastructure in the area. Fiber of course took over. There are some districts though, that still retain microwave redundancy for critical paths, realizing that our illustrious terra based providers suffer from backhoeitis. Systems started in the 1.6GHz band, then moved to 2, then 5, and finally higher to 80GHz in some locations. (That's generally 1g full duplex, though we did do a 10g hop for one customer.) Today, you can run 8g in 6GHz.... take that you WiFi wannabe's.

messages.author: [AL0R] Matt
messages.text: On the historical.... in the late 90's, Western Multiplex produce the Lynx and Tsunami point to point radios. The Tsunami GX90 was a 45 megabit full duplex unit that operated in the low 5GHz WiFi (UNII1 and 2) before the outdoor power limits applied. OPS deployed a dozen of these. The last one was removed just a few years ago, however, there are still a few GX90s running in the area. All ran at least 2' dishes, with most using 3's, but Burke had a 4' as did an endpoint at the Lucent building (Western Electric, AT&T, Lecent, Avaya, Commscope, etc.) While most WiFi bridges running outdoors came later and had to observe rather low power limits, when the GXs came out, they did not. The originals ran 36dbm (4W), whereas the later rules lowered this to 10dbm (10mw), and when coupled with a 4' dish (36db), produced an erp of ~12,000 watts
messages.content_html: On the historical.... in the late 90's, Western Multiplex produce the Lynx and Tsunami point to point radios. The Tsunami GX90 was a 45 megabit full duplex unit that operated in the low 5GHz WiFi (UNII1 and 2) before the outdoor power limits applied. OPS deployed a dozen of these. The last one was removed just a few years ago, however, there are still a few GX90s running in the area. All ran at least 2' dishes, with most using 3's, but Burke had a 4' as did an endpoint at the Lucent building (Western Electric, AT&T, Lecent, Avaya, Commscope, etc.) While most WiFi bridges running outdoors came later and had to observe rather low power limits, when the GXs came out, they did not. The originals ran 36dbm (4W), whereas the later rules lowered this to 10dbm (10mw), and when coupled with a 4' dish (36db), produced an erp of ~12,000 watts

messages.author: [AL0R] Matt
messages.text: Our area is awash in unlicensed usage. Schools, governments, commercial, all spray massive amounts of RF into the sky. The ISM bands are even worse, with so many devices in the 900 space for example. It's a downfall for things like Mesh where we are competing with higher powered deives, better antennas, and usually better placement.

messages.author: Rusty Chinook
messages.text: Yeah, looks like you can see my MC repeater. It is important to understand that the roles of Meshcore devices are different. Repeaters are basically dedicated devices, and companions are what most people would interact with using your computer or phone. This setup can tend to increase the amount of overall hardware that is required to grow the mesh. The chief benefit, as far as I can tell, is more reliable connections, or probably more precisely, the feedback that reliable network connections exist. I still operate MT as well because MC does not yet have the feature set I find most useful.
messages.content_html: Yeah, looks like you can see my MC repeater. It is important to understand that the roles of Meshcore devices are different. Repeaters are basically dedicated devices, and companions are what most people would interact with using your computer or phone. This setup can tend to increase the amount of overall hardware that is required to grow the mesh. The chief benefit, as far as I can tell, is more reliable connections, or probably more precisely, the feedback that reliable network connections exist. I still operate MT as well because MC does not yet have the feature set I find most useful.

messages.author: too3zln
messages.text: Do you think I should switch my t114 over to core? It could be more beneficial than staying on meshtastic. I guess only one way to find out.
messages.content_html: Do you think I should switch my t114 over to core? It could be more beneficial than staying on meshtastic. I guess only one way to find out.

messages.author: Rusty Chinook
messages.text: I would say that depends on your intended purpose. If you currently have good communication with your intended recipients using MT, they would have to switch along with you. There is a very good chance you won't be able to reach those devices from across town because there are few MC nodes (no others than my own that I am aware of) to repeat your messages. If you are just tinkering with no clear purpose, go for it may allow you to learn about how MC works, test range, etc.
messages.content_html: I would say that depends on your intended purpose. If you currently have good communication with your intended recipients using MT, they would have to switch along with you. If you are just tinkering with no clear purpose, go for it may allow you to learn about how MC works, test range, etc.

messages.author: Rusty Chinook
messages.text: Silly question that may have been answered before: in MT after sending a message in LongFast, a cloud with a check mark is an indication that the message was received by at least one other node. In MC Public channel, sometimes there is a "Heard 1 repeat" with a tick in a filled circle indicating that at least a repeater heard the message. Sometimes there is a "Sent" with a tick in a hollow circle. Does the tick indicate that the radio successfully transmitted and does the hollow circle signify it was not acknowledged? Would I get a "Sent" with a filled circle if the message had reached another node directly without an intervening repeater?
messages.content_html: Silly question that may have been answered before: in MT after sending a message in LongFast, a cloud with a check mark is an indication that the message was received by at least one other node. In MC Public channel, sometimes there is a "Heard 1 repeat" with a tick in a filled circle indicating that at least a repeater heard the message. Sometimes there is a "Sent" with a tick in a hollow circle. Does the tick indicate that the radio successfully transmitted and does the hollow circle signify it was not acknowledged? Would I get a "Sent" with a filled circle if the message had reached another node directly without an intervening repeater?

messages.author: Caboose
messages.text: So Meshcore has kind of a...dumb thing where it looks at the first 2 numbers of a repeaters public key and uses that for route tracing. When 2 repeaters have the same first 2 digits in the key it gets weird about displaying hops. It'll often say something went across multiple different hops at once leading to some confusion
messages.content_html: So Meshcore has kind of a...dumb thing where it looks at the first 2 numbers of a repeaters public key and uses that for route tracing. When 2 repeaters have the same first 2 digits in the key it gets weird about displaying hops. It'll often say something went across multiple different hops at once leading to some confusion

messages.author: Jonzy
messages.text: I had a reason to start playing with MT. It was to set up off grid coms with family. I was unable to achieve my goal over a 12 mile area in the city for many reasons. Too many hops and high usage on the freq seemed to top the list. I tried to move to Medium Slow and got rid of the chatter but couldn't span the distance because of the hills and valleys. MC has appeared to provide a more reliable way for communicating but it does change some of the bells and whistles I liked about MT. With the coordination of the MOB meshers, I think the infrastructure is building well.

messages.author: amc4550
messages.text: Yeah that makes sense. On my Rak without the amp I saw numerous instances of AGC lock from a nearby lora signal ~6mhz away. Then when running through the 2 way amp (which worked well on MT and is working well now on Heltec V2) it was pretty much in constant AGC lock and was still missing a bunch of packets even with a 4 second reset. Any idea if there is a way to completely disable AGC? It will miss any distant signals during strong local interference anyway, so I don't see the point in trying to quiet that down. If it's a receiver longevity issue... these are $30 devices. I'd be willing to roll the dice.

messages.author: PickleKing
messages.text: In my mind, either MT or MC could go on to make changes that put either one into a leading position. Nothing is stopping MT from implementing some alternate features in message routing that makes MC obsolete. But for now, MC is the clear winner for messaging. As far as this group is concerned, we’re suddenly hitting what feels like a critical mass moment. I vote we just keep moving on MC and focus on building the group more than an affiliation to a firmware. Then with a functioning group we could pivot back to MT if something big changes in the future. That’s my 2 cents. But I’m along for the ride. And if core repeaters like MEAD and a couple in omaha were to switch, then realistically I’ll be following due to the coverage.

messages.author: Rusty Chinook
messages.text: A different (very much minority) perspective: I had initially purchased LoRa equipment thinking more in terms of IoT type of network than a human-to-human communications network. MT seemed like a ready-built way to piggyback non-critical (ok if occasionally missed) IoT-type data over a free network that doesn't require a license and uses cheap hardware that interacts well with sensors and electronics that can be solar powered. A nice playground for extending the capabilities of arduinos and sensors for messaging non-critical data and for remote control. So from my view, the accessibility of long-range radio for non-critical communication and experimentation with other non-RF low-powered hardware is more important than critical person-to-person communication. MC is still in its early stages, so it will likely develop to incorporate more of that into this hobby. Until then, I will be keeping a foot in both worlds.

messages.author: Meaningless
messages.text: Ok, so since I am new to this stuff I need some advice. I talked to my uncle who lives on the north east side of Lincoln and he had no problem me putting up a node at his place. I would have regular access to this so I planning a typical solar node that I have seen multiple examples of but if there is any recommendation on specific parts to make it as maintenance free as possible that would be great.

messages.author: [AL0R] Matt
messages.text: Welcome to trying to support a very immature application. I support sites that have rare access as well. It is unfortunate that some of the "better" infrastructure nodes have the worst supportability. You want a node that you can update without pressing buttons, and ideally, one that supports OTA (and that is within range of wifi too.) On top of the water tower makes this all but impossible.
messages.content_html: Welcome to trying to support a very immature application. I support sites that have rare access as well. messages.author: [AL0R] Matt
messages.text: like everything... there is a LOT of variability. Indoor patch cable will degrade in UV, so anything outdoors needs to be protected. You also want physical protection from morons, I mean workers, so now you're to an aerial/armored option. Now you're in the buck a foot range. And you'll need to terminate it.... mechanicals are not recommended in vibratory environments, which the radio side is. Fusion splicer? nah, we'll do epoxy. You also need the ground side. Access "inside" the base of water towers is the same as topside. It's a challenge. They don't smile when you ask to drill a hole lol. So what was the point?
messages.content_html: like everything... there is a LOT of variability. Indoor patch cable will degrade in UV, so anything outdoors needs to be protected. You also want physical protection from morons, I mean workers, so now you're to an aerial/armored option. Now you're in the buck a foot range. And you'll need to terminate it.... mechanicals are not recommended in vibratory environments, which the radio side is. Fusion splicer? nah, we'll do epoxy. You also need the ground side. Access "inside" the base of water towers is the same as topside. It's a challenge. They don't smile when you ask to drill a hole lol. So what was the point?

messages.author: PickleKing
messages.text: It looks like it should connect to the broader mesh very well from that location. A couple notes on its location: first, it should connect to PT01 assuming it isn’t too far outside the directional wedge of that antenna. I think you’ll be good though. Second, there’s still a bit of elevation between Little John and the lower part of old town Bellevue. That should be overcome by the fact that it’s a short distance and by any height of another repeater down in Bellevue. But that elevation that impedes the literal line of sight is very near little John which may lead to asymmetric signal strength. Overall that’s a solid location that I think will solve the Bellevue issue.

messages.author: Rusty Chinook
messages.text: Imagine if you were one of those farmers at the top of that hill and you figured out that you could hook up a temperature/humidity sensor to a mesh repeater and stick it near the top of your grain bin for <$100...you know, just as a backup sensor for keeping an eye on moisture. Send yourself hourly data over a private channel to a companion connected to your desktop in your office that's just a bit too far for wifi. And the cherry on top...you've just added a link in a greater communication mesh that covers a much wider geographic area.

messages.author: Rusty Chinook
messages.text: It can be helpful if you try building your own antennas, like yagis and moxons, and want to tune them. A nice piece of kit to have in the toolbox. Be warned, that would put you at the threshold of a rabbit hole that is deep and wide. If you are just looking to see actual real-world performance of antennas you already have in hand, I have found actual field testing to be more helpful, especially, if while out and about doing other things, you can pull off the road to ping yourself and take a few notes .

messages.author: Scott Redd
messages.text: Your V4 max transmit power is 28 dBm, which is about 630 mW. The V3 and many other boards max power is 22 dBm, which is about 156 mW. 10 dBm is 10 mW, comparatively very low. Standard best practice for most RF systems is to use the lowest amount of power to achieve communications. A well placed repeater should probably use the maximum power possible. A companion that has a good path to a repeater could get by with less. I suspect most people just max out everything. Matt could probably explain this better.
messages.content_html: Your V4 max transmit power is 28 dBm, which is about 630 mW. The V3 and many other boards max power is 22 dBm, which is about 156 mW. 10 dBm is 10 mW, comparatively very low. Standard best practice for most RF systems is to use the lowest amount of power to achieve communications. A well placed repeater should probably use the maximum power possible. A companion that has a good path to a repeater could get by with less. I suspect most people just max out everything. Matt could probably explain this better.

messages.author: Scott Redd
messages.text: I think Meshtastic power settings were like this, where a TX setting was interpreted differently by different devices. I believe MeshCore firmware understands a device’s capabilities, so TX power settings are accurate. If you’ve got the device connected to a power amplifier, then you’d need to match the radio’s output to what the PA wants.
messages.content_html: I think Meshtastic power settings were like this, where a TX setting was interpreted differently by different devices. I believe MeshCore firmware understands a device’s capabilities, so TX power settings are accurate. If you’ve got the device connected to a power amplifier, then you’d need to match the radio’s output to what the PA wants.
messages.reply_html:
nightcrawler
10dbm might actually be about the same as 22dbm of others since it is 10dbm plus boost.

messages.author: Scott Redd
messages.text: MeshCore adverts are a one way broadcast. You could try Tools, Discover Nearby Nodes, Discover Repeaters, but I don’t always have luck. Many folks just transmit into the Public channel and look for repeats. In OmaMesh we’ve set up a #test channel so folks can test without cluttering Public. Repeaters will repeat any channel. There’s also an auto responder on #test that will let you know it hears if you send the word “test” or “testing”.

messages.author: nightcrawler
messages.forwarded_html:
Forwarded
Greetings earthlings! Here is a note to let everyone know that BirminghamMesh has moved away from Meshtastic, in favor of Meshcore. I am new to this server, but have been working with Meshcore (and Reticulum) for several months and really like what I see. About half of our existing mesh repeaters in high places have migrated as of earlier this morning, with the remaining core repeaters expected to migrate in the next few days/weeks. We had hoped to stick with the USA standard, but its Spread Factor of 7 would not work for the existing distances and hilly environment. Perhaps at some point in the future, we may move to SF 7 once additional repeaters come online to fill in the gaps. BhamMesh config params: 907.375 MHz, SF 9, CR 5 We picked the 907.375 MHz frequency after watching traffic over several days, but also because it falls within the passband of the already deployed RF cavities, which saves time and effort... Happy New Year!
Originally sent: 2025-12-28 6:33 p.m. (edited)

messages.author: Scott Redd
messages.text: Good point. Patience is a virtue. I changed it to: Your first steps on the mesh Once your device is configured: Power it on and let it run for a few minutes Watch for node discovery - you should start seeing nearby stations. It make take a few hours depending on the advert frequency of companion nodes, repeaters, and room servers. Find nearby repeaters: in your companion app, navigate to Tools -> Discover Nearby Nodes -> Discover Repeaters. This will ask local repeaters to identify themselves to you. Send a short message in the public channel and look for repeats or even replies from other users Try sending a direct message (DM) to another node Look for a delivery acknowledgement (ACK) If you get an ACK, congratulations - you are on the mesh. (edited)
messages.content_html: Good point. Patience is a virtue. messages.author: Scott Redd
messages.text: While Blair isn't really metro Omaha (neither in Lincoln), it's still important, and neat enough that it would be way cool to build out a larger mesh that bridges anything we can. I will add a page to the site at http://omamesh.net about mesh building in other areas in the region and hopefully folks looking for info online will come across it and join us.
messages.content_html: While Blair isn't really metro Omaha (neither in Lincoln), it's still important, and neat enough that it would be way cool to build out a larger mesh that bridges anything we can. I will add a page to the site at http://omamesh.net about mesh building in other areas in the region and hopefully folks looking for info online will come across it and join us.
embeds_html:
Home | OmaMesh
Metro Omaha mesh community on Meshtastic and MeshCore.

messages.author: timmo_3.14
messages.text: Be aware that any pokey solder joints can pretty easily pierce a lipo pouch. I did a "thing" this past weekend that made me run a build out of the house while pulling off wires. I recommend a piece of plastic sheeting between the rak and lipo . (edited)
messages.content_html: Be aware that any pokey solder joints can pretty easily pierce a lipo pouch. I did a "thing" this past weekend that made me run a build out of the house while pulling off wires. I recommend a piece of plastic sheeting between the rak and lipo . (edited)
messages.reactions_html: 2
messages.reply_html:
Scott Redd
It fits! Just barely. It’s tight enough I didn’t need any kind of insert to keep it from shifting inside. Had to orient the board plus JST in order to make proper use of the space. I made a polarity swapping cable and I’m sure my solder joints are sus.

messages.author: timmo_3.14
messages.text: I do know that the rak can only charge the battery at max 350mA. You can have more than that at the solar input, but max charging is 350mA.
messages.content_html: I do know that the rak can only charge the battery at max 350mA. You can have more than that at the solar input, but max charging is 350mA.
messages.reply_html:
Scott Redd
Gotta double check the power requirements of the solar connector though. I think it’s pretty forgiving.

messages.author: Scott Redd
messages.text: But it won't hurt it thought, right? If I tie on a 0.5A 5v feed into the solar port, it'll just regulate to 350?
messages.content_html: But it won't hurt it thought, right? If I tie on a 0.5A 5v feed into the solar port, it'll just regulate to 350?

messages.author: timmo_3.14
messages.text: Correct. I had a 1A 5v (5 watt) and it was fine - made sure I had that max charge on cloudy days. (edited)
messages.content_html: Correct. I had a 1A 5v (5 watt) and it was fine - made sure I had that max charge on cloudy days. (edited)
